title: Model Routing by Query Difficulty
concept: Sending easy queries to a small/cheap model and only escalating hard queries to a larger, costlier one.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we discussed prompt caching and compression. What primary goal do these techniques support?

options.text: Reducing generation costs in production LLM apps
Replacing all LLMs with traditional SQL databases

exercises.explain: Prompt caching, context compression, and routing simple queries to cheaper models are all core strategies to reduce the overall cost of generating model responses.

cards.title: Smart Routing, Less Spending
cards.body: Why pay premium prices for a giant AI model just to answer 'hello' or 'what time is it'? We can save massive amounts of money by routing easy tasks to smaller models.

cards.title: What is Model Routing?
cards.body: Model routing is the process of using a fast, cheap classifier (or simple rules) to inspect an incoming prompt and decide whether to send it to a small, inexpensive model or a large, powerful one.

cards.title: The Receptionist Analogy
cards.body: Think of routing like a customer service desk. The receptionist handles simple requests like 'where is the restroom?' instantly, but forwards complex accounts questions to the manager.

cards.title: Routing in Action
cards.body: If a user asks 'What is 2+2?', the router sends it to a tiny model costing pennies. If the user asks for a '10-page python code refactor', the router sends it to the flagship model.

cards.title: Myth: Routers Must Be Heavy
cards.body: You don't need a massive, expensive LLM to route your traffic. A simple keyword filter, a tiny classifier model, or even prompt length checks are often enough to make the routing decision.

cards.title: Dividing the Workload
cards.body: Compare how queries are handled based on their difficulty level to optimize speed and budget.

left.text: Routed to small, fast, ultra-cheap model.

right.text: Routed to large, smart, premium model.

exercises.prompt: How does a model router decide where to send an incoming user query?

options.text: It duplicates the query and sends it to all models simultaneously.
It evaluates the query difficulty first and forwards it to the cheapest capable model.
It always routes every query to the most expensive model to ensure quality.
It translates the query into multiple languages before processing.

exercises.explain: A cheap classifier or rule-based system inspects the query first and decides which model is best suited to handle it, balancing cost and performance.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Sending basic greetings to a small model instead of a giant model is an effective way to optimize costs.
exercises.explain: Simple queries like FAQ lookups or greeting responses can be easily handled by small models, saving the expensive model for complex reasoning.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the description of cost-effective query flow:
exercises.sentence: By analyzing the query first, we make a smart routing decision to save costs.
exercises.explain: We check the query first to make a routing decision, ensuring we don't send simple greetings to a massive, expensive model.

exercises.prompt: Which of these logs represents an automated model routing system in production?

options.text: Our router decided to send the math problem to the cheap model because it wanted to save money, even though the user will get a wrong answer.
[Router Log] Query: 'Hi there' -> Intent: Greeting (Low Difficulty) -> Routed to: small-model-v2 (Cost: $0.00001)
We manually review every single user query in real time and press a physical button to route it.

exercises.explain: Option B shows a realistic automated routing log where a simple intent classifier redirects a basic greeting to a lightweight, cheap model.

exercises.prompt: Why is dynamic model routing considered a key strategy for cost optimization in production LLM applications?

options.text: It reduces the speed of simple queries by forcing them through complex reasoning steps.
It balances cost and quality by using cheap models for easy tasks and saving expensive models for hard ones.
It completely eliminates the need to use large models for any task.
It automatically compresses all input texts to zero tokens.

exercises.explain: Evaluating query difficulty before sending it to an LLM prevents wasting money on simple tasks while ensuring complex tasks still get the high-quality reasoning they need from a larger model.
